one_liner: A 32-year-old pregnant woman in her third trimester awoke this morning unable to close her right eye or smile on the right. She had an upper respiratory illness two weeks ago.

stage2.hpi_extension: She noticed mild posterior auricular pain yesterday. This morning, when brushing her teeth, toothpaste dribbled out of the right side of her mouth. She cannot **raise her right eyebrow, wrinkle her forehead, close her right eye, or smile** on the right. She reports **sounds seem abnormally loud in her right ear (hyperacusis)** and **decreased taste on the front part of her tongue on the right**. No limb weakness, no vertigo, no hearing loss, no rash or vesicles. She had an upper respiratory infection with sore throat and mild fever about 2 weeks ago that resolved on its own.
stage2.pmh: Pregnancy, currently at 32 weeks gestation, otherwise unremarkable
stage2.social_history: Teacher. No tobacco or alcohol during pregnancy.
stage2.physical_exam: **Right-sided facial droop involving the entire right half of the face, including the forehead**. Unable to wrinkle the right forehead, unable to fully close the right eye (on attempted closure the eye rolls upward — **Bell phenomenon**), flattened right nasolabial fold, drooping corner of the mouth on the right. Cranial nerves II, III, IV, V, VI, VIII, IX, X, XI, XII intact. **No limb weakness, no sensory deficit, no cerebellar signs, no dysarthria, no dysphagia**. **No vesicles in the external auditory canal or on the pinna** (no Ramsay Hunt features). Otoscopy normal. The rest of the exam is normal.

lab_results.result: MRI brain with gadolinium (deferred in pregnancy but performed due to diagnostic uncertainty): **enhancement of the right facial nerve within the internal auditory canal and labyrinthine segment** without mass lesion. No brainstem stroke, no cerebellopontine angle mass, no demyelinating lesion.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Facial nerve enhancement is a nonspecific finding but supports an inflammatory/infectious etiology of a peripheral CN VII palsy. Imaging is mainly used to exclude central causes (stroke) or structural lesions (tumor, cerebellopontine angle mass) when the presentation is atypical.

lab_results.result: Sodium 138, glucose 94, creatinine 0.6, LFTs normal. HbA1c 5.2%.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal glucose rules out undiagnosed diabetes, which is a known risk factor for Bell palsy.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on **oral prednisone within 48 hours of symptom onset** (safe in pregnancy), given **artificial tears and nighttime eye lubrication with an eye patch** to prevent exposure keratitis, and counseled that **most patients recover fully within weeks to months**.

debrief.classic_presentation: Bell palsy is an **acute, idiopathic, unilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy** that develops over hours to a day. Because it is a **lower motor neuron lesion of CN VII**, it causes **weakness of the entire half of the face, including the forehead**, in contrast to a central (UMN) CN VII lesion, which spares the forehead due to bilateral cortical innervation of the upper face. Associated features reflect the course of the facial nerve: **hyperacusis** (loss of stapedius reflex), **decreased taste from the anterior two thirds of the tongue** (chorda tympani), **decreased lacrimation and salivation**, and retroauricular pain. Risk factors include **pregnancy (especially third trimester), diabetes mellitus, and preceding viral illness**. The differential includes stroke (sparing the forehead), Ramsay Hunt syndrome (vesicles in the ear canal from herpes zoster), Lyme disease (bilateral facial palsy in an endemic area), sarcoidosis, and parotid tumors.
debrief.pathophysiology: Bell palsy is thought to be due to **inflammation and edema of CN VII as it passes through the narrow bony facial canal of the temporal bone**, causing nerve compression and ischemic demyelination. The inflammation is likely triggered by reactivation of **latent herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1)** in the geniculate ganglion, though other viruses (VZV, EBV, CMV) have been implicated. Pregnancy (particularly third trimester), diabetes, and immunosuppression are risk factors. Axonal injury, when present, predicts a slower and sometimes incomplete recovery.
debrief.treatment_overview: **Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 60 mg daily x 5-7 days, tapered) started within 72 hours of onset** improve the likelihood and speed of recovery and are the mainstay of therapy. **Antivirals (acyclovir, valacyclovir)** are sometimes added in severe cases, though their additional benefit is modest. **Eye protection** is critical: **artificial tears, lubricating ointment, and a protective eye patch or taping the eye shut at night** prevent exposure keratitis and corneal ulceration. Most patients recover completely within weeks to months; ~70-85% regain full or near-full function. Failure to improve should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis and workup for alternative causes (Lyme, sarcoidosis, tumor).
high_yield_pearls: Bell palsy = peripheral CN VII palsy that affects the entire half of the face, INCLUDING the forehead — central CN VII (e.g., stroke) spares the forehead.
Associated features follow the course of CN VII: hyperacusis (stapedius), loss of taste from anterior 2/3 tongue (chorda tympani), decreased lacrimation.
Risk factors: pregnancy (especially third trimester), diabetes, and recent viral upper respiratory infection (HSV-1 reactivation hypothesis).
Treat with oral prednisone started within 72 hours; add eye lubrication and a patch to prevent exposure keratitis.
Bilateral facial palsy — think Lyme disease, sarcoidosis (Heerfordt syndrome), Guillain-Barré syndrome.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Cranial nerves — Facial nerve lesions / Bell palsy' section.
